It's like the small things that make a big difference in the conversation. So you know, when people sit down with a client and they want to understand them better and listen to their story and their history, sometimes people feel, and I know that it sounds like with therapy used to feel like you had to sit and listen to long, long stories unbroken where you're just kind of passively listening. But the truth is here, Tony is guiding the conversation. And the reason for doing this is that when people have a big story that they want to share, so in Joaquin's case, it's a story of disappointment and betrayal. It's important to understand, you know, all of the stories, not just that story. You want to hear the positive stories, you want to hear the negative stories, you want to hear the stories of the background, you want to hear the story about how Joaquin and Kim met. And so when you understand these stories, you have then a variety of stories that you can choose from when you make your reference to them. So you know, they may have a big, difficult story, but then Tony will point out, yes, over the last year, that's been a very difficult story, but look at all the other stories you have. And that gives you more resources as a coach. Let me answer that, something that I think is really important because it's a question that many people ask. Many people ask why in the interventions in front of a large audience, Tony doesn't want to go into the story and gets the facts very briefly. And here you're going to see in this first half of the round table that Tony listens very carefully to the whole traumatic event, to everything exactly that happened the day that Joaquin had the stroke. And he listens to every detail of the trauma because that was probably the most traumatic time in Joaquin's life. And so when Tony doesn't have to fulfill the needs of the large audience, he definitely lets the person explain their story. Absolutely. So if you have a client of any age who doesn't want to go to school or doesn't want to go to work, doesn't want to get a job, doesn't want to see anyone, the possibility here is that the person has had a major disappointment. Yeah. And so in Joaquin's case, you have someone who was living a successful life with a kind of status quo, like he was living his dream of being a pro basketball player, working hard, doing well, and then he has a setback. So initially it's a setback with his health, with his stroke, but he was okay with that. He was able to summon all of his energy and discipline and overcome that and get back on the basketball court, which is amazing in and of itself. And from a coach observing, when you see a client dealing effectively with adversity like Joaquin did, you know that that stroke in Joaquin's worldview was still okay with his model of the world. So he was able to cope with that and respond, but then getting fired from the team was not okay with it from his perspective. When he lost that, it was so overwhelming that he went into shock. And when people go into shock, they're no longer responsive to the new situation. So one way that people go into shock is that they're waiting for the world to reset. They're waiting for the earlier circumstances to come back. And so if it's a divorce, they're waiting for that spouse to call them. If they're got fired, they're waiting for someone to apologize and call them back and say, you can have your job back. And when people, I mean, when someone has someone die, they'll often have dreams where the person comes back and says, no, I'm not dead. I was just a misunderstanding. It's all okay. There's a part of our unconscious that comes from childhood that we believe that we just wait or close our eyes and ears or something or somehow things are going to come right for us again. And a kid who doesn't like what's happening will actually close their eyes and ears and shut out everything, waiting for a parent or someone to come and take care of them again so they can feel comfortable and have that baseline so that they can do what they were doing before. So Joaquin was in that kind of shock who's waiting for that call back and that reset button. And from the six human needs perspective, what we know is happening to the person in this case Joaquin, he's returned some of his base needs for certainty. As a coach, one of the things you look out for when someone is in this condition in this state is to see how are they taking care of themselves, their family, their home, their relationships, their spirit, their material possessions and so on. One thing about Joaquin, which is interesting to point out, what he does later in the session, is that I think actually in a session that you guys weren't supposed to see this week, you may have, it's further on in the round table, is that he points out how strong Joaquin still is. He's still fit, he's still working out, he still has big muscles. So that's an area where he hasn't given up hope. Joaquin is also able to talk about who he was in the past and what he believed in. And he talks about the past in such a believable way that it's really hard at times, I think as the observer watching this session, to believe that Joaquin actually lost hope, that he was actually so depressed and not working and not even opening up his bills because when he talks about the past, it's just so believable. Yeah. So when you have a client who's in shock, one of the things to discover is which areas of their lives or that is a person still operational and where have they actually shut down. So as a coach, you want to look at the situation and think, okay, this person's in shock, they're disappointed, they're trying to cope, they're no longer responsive to the real world or no longer responsive to the current circumstances, but what are they responding to, what is still active in the world? Because that will show you what the person still has hope for and what they're holding out for. Yeah. So you want to know, are they opening up their bills? Are they still active in their friendship? Do they still have sex? Do they exercise? Do they cook for themselves? Do they clean their house? Do they read books or do they read on the Internet? Do they read the newspaper? Do they check their emails? These may seem like really basic questions, but it really tells you a lot about how much they've shut down or shut out. And that gives you the information of the coach to know where you need to reach out. So for example, later we'll talk about the community session, not today, but in the future that Tony does with Joaquin's family and community. But if a client was still in very active communication with their siblings, with their good friends, with the people in their life, the coach wouldn't necessarily have to reach out to all those people at that kind of level because there would already be a level of truth and understanding in the client's circle of relationships and community. In this case, Joaquin and his wife made it very clear that there were a lot of secrets, a lot of holding on to an image of success and of their placement in the community where they were givers and they were looked up to. So they weren't being truthful about their situation with their community. So truthfulness is a very important part of the work with Joaquin from the big picture. Personally, and as he related to his wife, his kids, his uncle, his friends, his mother, his coach, truth was really important. And one of the reasons why truth was so important specifically in this case is that it really goes to Joaquin's being a team player, a member of a team, and also his huge sense of commitment and responsibility to take care of his wife, to take care of his mother, to take care of his daughters. And so by looking at the list of what your client is or isn't doing or watching out for indications of what they're doing or not doing, where they're inactive and when they're active is you learn what the client's holding out for. So one of the principles of coaching is that you want to really understand your client's deepest desires because that is where the energy is that will create growth and make things happen and change for them. So people have this desire when they're really productive and moving forward, but they also have this desire when they're disappointed and paralyzed. It's the desire, that's frustrated desire that creates these problems very often, makes people give up hope because they have that desire there. So you want to find that and dig and find that. And so sometimes when someone is disappointed and in shock that they're not ready to let go of an expectation that they used to have. So Joaquin expected to be a basketball player and when that was taken away from him, he wasn't ready to let go of that. And by the way, there's nothing wrong with having expectations. I know sometimes people talk about you shouldn't have expectations. We believe very practical. Expectations are important. They drive us to achievement. They drive us to high standards. They guide our lives. For Joaquin, when the external world stopped supporting his expectations, he became unresponsive to the new situation. And so as a coach, you want to understand what they're holding out for. And one way to see this is to see when they're shut down and where they're still operational. So Joaquin is working out, but he's not opening his bills or getting a job or telling anyone what's going on. The truth is that he's holding out and hoping that he still gets back on the team. And they get an apology from the coach and that the right is wrong. The wrong is right. And he could have gone in any other direction. It's good to ask yourself also, which other direction could my client have gone? Another client may have said, they're never calling me back. I might as well start drinking and overrating and partying and whatever. He didn't do that. He took care of himself because he was ready for that callback. He's still holding onto that idea. So when Tony guesses that for Joaquin, being on a team is really important and that it's part of his mode of being. And we'll talk about what is a mode of being? We use this term to describe the way a person relates to the world, their identity, their relationship, their goals. It's kind of like a metaphor, but it's really broader. It's about how you have your identity and relate to the things and the people that you care about. Yeah, it's a way you process information at the deepest level. So if you've gone to the place where you're prominent in a sports team, chances are that being on a team is an important mode of being for you. But on the other hand, for someone who's a journalist or an observer or a questioner, that's a huge part of their identity and mode of being. Very different from being on a team. If you're a teacher or a philosopher thinking, relating information, giving in that way, that's a part of your identity. So when we learn about our client's deeper mode of being, when we talk to them about their career, when we ask them to tell us their story of how they got to their career or the story of their childhood. And someone tells you, I'm a teacher for a living, but my passion is to paint. And my best moments are when I'm alone painting in nature. Then we know a lot right there. Their bigger mode of being is to observe, to appreciate, and to feel union with nature. And something larger than them in the act of painting. And that's really different from someone whose preferred mode of being is to be a teacher or someone who's a team player. So it's a really great thing as a coach to understand the mode of being that your client chooses to work with in the world. And you'll see in Joaquin's case, this was so important because with his disappointment, what was also missing was being part of a team. And so Tony guided him to be part of another team with much bigger challenges than a sports team. And you know, at first listening to Joaquin, you could think really he needs significance, he needs love and fans. So as a strategic interventionist, you're working with someone who's been disappointed in this shock, then the story that they're going to have, which is about people when they're having that story, it's about things that are out of their control. And what's out of their control? It's basically other people in the past and things that are out of your reach. So they focus on the other people, what they did to me, how they didn't give me a chance and on the past, all the things that happen, all the tragic things that happen that can't be changed now. That's an external blame focus. Now the thing about a mode of being is that you can always reengage that person's mode of being, even without all those external factors being in place. So as a strategic interventionist, you want to find that person's mode of being the way that they comfortably, positively, happily are engaged in activities that are meaningful and express their values and whatever they, and their desires. And then whether they're a team player or an investigator or an artist or an inventor, these are all different modes of being. You figure out what that is, you locate that mode of being so they can get into it again, even if they have completely different circumstances. So Tony's goal was to make Joaquin a part of the team, whether or not he could be part of a basketball team. What Tony did, he created this challenge is to engage Joaquin's mode of being in completely different circumstances, but being able to make it work. And the last story Tony asked Joaquin and Kim to share is the story of the stroke. And what I think is really interesting is that Tony asked this by saying, take me to the day that altered your life so drastically. So notice he isn't saying, tell me the story. Instead, he's asking to be taken to the day. And as a coach, if you ask a client in this way, what's happening? I think what you're doing is you're going there to that day with them. Now that you're there with them, you have the ability to elevate that day and those events and to see the story from one of the other lenses. There's so many different lenses to view any day or story. And if you notice Tony keeps asking Kim how she felt that day. He asked maybe four times. And I think what Tony is doing is aligning both Joaquin and Kim with their emotions. Instead of the facts, the terrible facts, the terrible mistakes and the blame. He's aligning them with the emotions, which they have control over. Yeah. So it's a great strategy when you have someone with a big story. Is that the client will have a story. And by the way, many coaches think, I never want to hear your story. Don't give me your story. And that's a mistake. Because if the client, you know, the client is paying you to help to understand them. If you don't want to even hear their story, they're going to be feel like I'm going to find someone who's going to understand me. You know, so you want to understand the story. You want to work skillfully with the story. So when people have a story like this, they have painful generalizations and conclusions that they've drawn. Now generalizations tend to be highly emotional. Why? Because generalizations cover more information. So what's more intense to say? To say my life sucks or to say this morning sucks. You know, my life sucks is more intense because it's more general. Talks about your entire life and everything inside your life is contained in that generalization. It's way more intense. Right. So when Tony is working with them to understand the story, it's to break it down and to break it down so that it's like Magalie said, separating emotions from facts. How did you feel versus what happened? Because people like to make the conclusion that because this thing happened, my feeling is completely justified. Right. And another one is breaking down the getting very concrete. Who's communicating with who you're going to see as you as you get further into the exploration of what actually happened to Joaquin, especially when they talk to the coach is that they drew all these painful generalizations and once they get more concrete, they realized, oh, this person wasn't communicated this. This person didn't wasn't aware of that. This thing happened not because of what they did, but because of how you responded. And you see all the contingencies and the nitty gritty.
